statement: RSS 2.0 and Atom 1.0 are the standard feed formats for content syndication. feedparser (Python) handles both formats plus various extension modules (iTunes, Dublin Core, GeoRSS). UNSAID: many feeds are malformed XML — feedparser's lenient parsing handles most real-world feeds, but strict XML parsers will fail. Feed update frequency must be respected: polling too frequently gets IPs banned. If-Modified-Since and ETag HTTP headers enable conditional fetching — skip parsing if feed unchanged. Extraction signal: info ingestion projects that poll all feeds on the same schedule regardless of their update frequency waste resources and risk IP bans.

Web scraping stack: requests/httpx for HTTP, BeautifulSoup/lxml for HTML parsing, Playwright/Selenium for JavaScript-rendered pages. Scrapy is a full framework for structured scraping with pipelines, middlewares, and item processors. UNSAID: robots.txt is a legal and ethical constraint, not just a suggestion in many jurisdictions. User-Agent string should identify the bot, not impersonate a browser — unless intentionally bypassing detection, which may violate ToS. Rate limiting with exponential backoff and jitter prevents overloading target servers.

Content deduplication prevents redundant storage and processing of duplicate articles. URL normalization (remove UTM params, canonicalize scheme/path) is the first pass. Content fingerprinting (SimHash, MinHash) detects near-duplicate content with different URLs. UNSAID: the same article often appears at multiple URLs (AMP version, syndicated copies, mobile/desktop). A fingerprint-based dedup is more effective than URL dedup alone. Extraction signal: info ingestion projects without dedup will balloon storage and surface duplicate content to users.

Info ingestion systems must respect rate limits of upstream sources: News APIs (NewsAPI, Guardian, NYT) have per-day request limits. RSS/Atom fetching should use conditional HTTP (ETag/If-Modified-Since) and back off on 429 responses. UNSAID: API rate limits are often enforced per key and per IP — running multiple workers with the same API key counts as one entity for rate limiting purposes. Using Redis-based rate limiting (redis-py-rate-limiter) centralizes limit state across workers. Extraction signal: multi-worker scrapers without centralized rate limiting will quickly exhaust API quotas.

Extracting the main article text from HTML (removing navigation, ads, sidebars) is a key challenge. trafilatura and newspaper3k are Python libraries for this. Mozilla's Readability algorithm (also available as a Python port) is used by Firefox Reader Mode. UNSAID: ML-based extractors (trafilatura uses heuristics + ML) outperform CSS-selector approaches for arbitrary websites. Hardcoded CSS selectors break when site templates change. For structured extraction (specific fields), use a combination of structured data (JSON-LD, OpenGraph) first, then fall back to heuristic extraction. Extraction signal: scrapers using brittle CSS selectors will require constant maintenance.

Info ingestion pipelines need a work queue for: fetch jobs, processing jobs, and notification jobs. Celery + Redis/RabbitMQ is the classic Python task queue. ARQ (asyncio-based, simpler) is modern alternative. UNSAID: Celery's default settings have serious problems: (1) result backend stores ALL results forever unless expires= is set, filling Redis; (2) task_acks_late=False means failed tasks are lost if the worker crashes mid-execution; (3) no retry by default — transient failures cause permanent task loss. Extraction signal: Celery without max_retries and retry_on_exception configured loses tasks silently.

robots.txt is both an ethical and increasingly a legal constraint on web scraping. The Robots Exclusion Protocol specifies which paths crawlers are disallowed from accessing, and Disallow rules should be respected by any compliant bot. UNSAID: robots.txt is fetched from the root of each domain before any crawling begins and should be cached (with TTL respect) to avoid excessive requests. User-agent: * applies to all bots; specific user-agent entries override the wildcard. Crawl-delay directive (non-standard but widely used) specifies seconds between requests — violating it is a common source of IP bans. The Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (US) and similar laws in other jurisdictions have been used against scrapers who bypass explicit technical restrictions. Beyond legality, aggressive crawling can DDoS small sites — always use a crawl rate that represents a negligible fraction of the target's traffic. Extraction signal: scraping pipelines that fetch robots.txt only once at build time will miss updates and accumulate legal/ethical debt.

Data normalization transforms heterogeneous ingested content into a consistent canonical schema. Key normalization steps: Unicode normalization (NFC form), whitespace collapsing, HTML entity decoding, timezone normalization to UTC, URL canonicalization (lowercase, strip fragments, normalize encoding). UNSAID: publication date normalization is particularly treacherous — sources use ISO 8601, RFC 2822, Unix timestamps, informal formats like 'yesterday', and inconsistent timezones. Use dateparser (Python) or date-fns (JS) for robust multi-format parsing, but always validate the output year is plausible (sources sometimes emit epoch 0 or future dates). Character encoding detection with chardet/charset-normalizer is necessary for feeds that omit Content-Type charset declarations. Extraction signal: news aggregators that store raw publication dates as strings without timezone normalization produce incorrect chronological sorting.

Semantic deduplication goes beyond URL and fingerprint matching to identify articles covering the same event from different sources. Sentence transformers (all-MiniLM-L6-v2 from HuggingFace) produce dense embeddings; cosine similarity above a threshold (typically 0.85-0.92) indicates semantic duplicates. UNSAID: threshold tuning is critical — too low collapses distinct articles on related topics into one cluster; too high misses paraphrased duplicates. Approximate nearest neighbor search (FAISS, Annoy, hnswlib) makes similarity search feasible at millions of documents by avoiding brute-force O(n²) comparison. A hybrid approach combining MinHash LSH (fast, exact duplicate) → embedding similarity (slower, semantic) as a two-pass pipeline achieves both speed and quality. Extraction signal: aggregators showing 'cluster' or 'story grouping' features that only deduplicate by URL will show many near-duplicate stories from wire services (AP, Reuters syndication).

Incremental sync patterns keep a local copy in sync with a remote source without re-fetching everything. Strategies in order of preference: (1) cursor/watermark — store the highest seen ID or timestamp, fetch only newer items; (2) ETag/If-Modified-Since — HTTP conditional GET returns 304 Not Modified if unchanged; (3) webhook/push — source notifies on change; (4) full re-fetch with deduplication — brute force fallback. UNSAID: cursors based on timestamps are fragile because sources may back-fill historical articles with old timestamps, causing items to fall below the cursor and be missed permanently. ID-based cursors are safer when available. Webhooks are ideal but require the ingestion service to be publicly reachable and the source to support them — most news sources don't. Always store the raw cursor value in persistent storage, not in memory, to survive process restarts. Extraction signal: ingestion pipelines that restart from the beginning on process restart re-fetch and re-process years of history, wasting resources and triggering dedup logic unnecessarily.

API polling vs webhooks represents a fundamental architectural choice in info ingestion. Polling is simpler (no public endpoint required, works with any source) but wastes quota on unchanged data. Webhooks are event-driven (near real-time, no wasted calls) but require a stable public HTTPS endpoint and the source must support them. UNSAID: for sources that support both, prefer webhooks for latency-sensitive use cases and polling as a reliability backstop (missed webhooks are real — delivery is not guaranteed). Polling interval should match the source's update frequency: polling a daily-updated report every minute wastes 1439 out of 1440 requests. Server-Sent Events (SSE) and WebSockets are streaming alternatives to polling for sources that support them. Extraction signal: ingestion systems that poll high-frequency sources every second under a tight rate limit will exhaust their quota without getting meaningful incremental data.

Data quality scoring assigns a confidence or completeness score to ingested items, enabling downstream consumers to filter low-quality content. Dimensions to score: completeness (required fields present), freshness (publication date recency), source authority (domain reputation score), content length (too short = boilerplate or error page), language confidence (langdetect/fasttext), and extraction quality (did the main content extractor succeed). UNSAID: a single composite quality score hides which dimension failed — store per-dimension scores to enable targeted debugging. Quality thresholds should be configurable, not hardcoded, because acceptable quality varies by downstream use case: a monitoring system may accept low-quality items for completeness, while a recommendation engine should only serve high-quality content. Extraction signal: info pipelines without quality scoring serve error pages, boilerplate, and bot-trap content to users transparently.

Schema-on-read vs schema-on-write is a foundational architectural choice for ingested data storage. Schema-on-write enforces structure at ingest time (relational DB, strongly-typed columns), rejecting malformed data immediately. Schema-on-read stores raw/semi-structured data (JSON columns, document stores, data lakes) and applies structure at query time. UNSAID: schema-on-write is safer for production systems because malformed data never enters the store, but it requires upfront schema design and migration overhead when source formats change. News sources frequently change their APIs and feed schemas without notice — a strict schema will start dropping items silently when a required field is renamed. A hybrid approach: store the raw payload (schema-on-read) plus extract typed columns for indexed fields (schema-on-write for query fields). Use PostgreSQL JSONB for this hybrid pattern — it allows GIN indexing of JSON fields while keeping the raw payload queryable. Extraction signal: ingestion pipelines that fail hard on any schema violation will silently drop articles when upstream sources evolve.

Structured extraction from unstructured text uses a pipeline of techniques in order of reliability: (1) JSON-LD structured data in HTML head (most reliable, machine-generated); (2) OpenGraph and Twitter Card meta tags; (3) microdata/RDFa markup; (4) heuristic extraction (trafilatura, newspaper3k); (5) LLM-assisted extraction as a fallback. UNSAID: JSON-LD (schema.org/Article, schema.org/NewsArticle) is the gold standard — Google's structured data guidelines have incentivized publishers to embed accurate machine-readable metadata. However, JSON-LD data can be stale or incorrect even on the same page. LLM extraction is the most flexible but adds latency, cost, and hallucination risk — validate LLM-extracted fields against constraints (e.g., extracted date must be a valid ISO date, extracted author must appear somewhere in the document). Extraction signal: scrapers that ignore JSON-LD and immediately use heuristic extraction are discarding the most reliable signal on the page.

Change detection determines whether a previously ingested item has been updated (corrections, retractions, updates to developing stories). Strategies: compare content hash of the current crawl vs stored hash; diff the extracted text to detect meaningful changes vs whitespace changes; use HTTP Last-Modified header if reliable. UNSAID: many CMS platforms update the Last-Modified timestamp on every cache purge even if content didn't change — making it an unreliable change signal. Semantic diff (sentence-level similarity before/after) is more reliable than byte-level hash diff for detecting meaningful editorial changes. Tracking changes is important for trust: a news article quietly correcting a factual error without a correction notice is a signal of low source quality. Versioning changed articles (storing multiple versions with diff metadata) enables audit trails. Extraction signal: ingestion systems that only store the first version of an article miss corrections and retractions, serving stale content indefinitely.

Content fingerprinting detects near-duplicate content across different sources or URLs. Simhash (used by Google) represents a document as a 64-bit hash where similar documents have similar hashes — Hamming distance ≤ 3 indicates near-duplicates. MinHash + LSH (Locality-Sensitive Hashing) is better for finding similar documents in large corpora without pairwise comparison. UNSAID: fingerprinting must be applied to the extracted main content, not the raw HTML — identical articles served on different sites have different HTML (different navigation, ads, headers) but similar content fingerprints. Fingerprint computation should happen at ingest time and be stored alongside the item, not computed at query time. A critical failure mode: fingerprinting the title only (fast but wrong) misses syndicated articles with slightly different titles. TLDR summaries and translated versions of the same article will NOT match fingerprints — they require semantic embedding similarity. Extraction signal: aggregators showing the same Reuters or AP story from 15 different publications as separate items lack content-level deduplication.

Archival strategies for ingested content must balance storage cost, retrieval speed, and retention compliance. Hot storage (PostgreSQL, Elasticsearch) for recent content (last 30 days) with full-text search; warm storage (S3-compatible object storage, Parquet files) for 30 days–2 years; cold storage (Glacier, tape) for compliance archives. UNSAID: right-to-be-forgotten (GDPR Article 17) requires that personal data can be deleted from all storage tiers including backups — content stores that don't track which articles contain personal data cannot comply. Object storage tiering (S3 Intelligent-Tiering, Glacier Instant Retrieval) automates cost optimization by moving objects between storage classes based on access patterns. Columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) compress text data 5-10x compared to JSON and enable predicate pushdown for analytical queries. Extraction signal: ingestion systems storing all raw HTML indefinitely in a relational database will accumulate terabytes of storage cost without a tiering policy.

Incremental RSS/Atom feed parsing must handle the full RFC 4287 (Atom) and RSS 2.0 spec edge cases that real-world feeds exploit. Common failure modes: (1) feeds that omit <id>/<guid> forcing URL-based deduplication; (2) feeds where <updated> reflects the feed generation time, not article update time; (3) feeds that include only the 10 most recent items, silently dropping articles if the polling interval exceeds publication frequency; (4) feeds with escaped HTML in <content:encoded> requiring double-decoding; (5) feeds using relative URLs for <link> elements. UNSAID: feedparser handles many of these gracefully, but if rolling your own parser, each of these is a silent data loss bug. Pagination (RFC 5005 — Feed Paging and Archiving) allows feeds to expose historical pages, but almost no publisher implements it. The safest model is to store all seen GUIDs and never rely on feed ordering or completeness guarantees. Extraction signal: ingestion pipelines that only process the diff between the current and previous feed fetch will miss articles published and removed between two polling cycles.

Web scraping at scale requires a layered architecture separating URL discovery, fetch scheduling, content extraction, and storage. Fetcher tier handles HTTP with proper rate limiting, retry with exponential backoff, and rotating proxies. Extraction tier parses HTML with multiple strategies (CSS selectors, XPath, readability algorithms) falling back through a priority stack. Storage tier deduplicates before writing using content fingerprints. The key design principle: each tier should be independently scalable and independently replaceable. UNSAID: the most common architectural mistake is tightly coupling the fetcher and extractor in a single synchronous process, which makes it impossible to retry extraction without re-fetching and impossible to apply improved extractors to historically fetched content. Storing raw HTML alongside extracted content enables re-processing when extraction logic improves—critical for ML training data collection where ground truth labels evolve.

JavaScript-rendered content (SPAs, lazy-loaded elements) is invisible to HTTP-only scrapers—fetching the URL returns a nearly empty HTML skeleton, not the rendered content. Headless browser scraping (Playwright, Puppeteer) is required but comes with 5-10x higher cost per page and complex anti-detection requirements. UNSAID: many sites that appear to require JavaScript actually load content via XHR/fetch APIs—inspecting network tab often reveals JSON API endpoints returning the data directly, accessible without headless browser overhead. GraphQL introspection queries can expose private API endpoints. A hybrid architecture routes URLs through a classifier that predicts JavaScript requirement, using HTTP for most and headless browser only for confirmed SPA targets. Playwright's response interception (page.route()) can capture the underlying API calls made by the page, extracting structured data even from complex SPAs.

Distributed crawl scheduling with URL frontier management: implement priority queues using Redis sorted sets (ZADD with score=priority*timestamp) for real-time politeness. Per-domain queues enforce crawl delay: store last_fetch_time per domain in Redis, block fetch if time_since_last < crawl_delay. Bloom filter (pybloomfiltermmap) for visited URL deduplication at billion-URL scale—Bloom filter false positive rate 0.1% is acceptable for deduplication. Canonical URL normalization before insertion: sort query params, strip tracking params (utm_*, fbclid, gclid), resolve relative URLs, lowercase scheme and host. Store frontier state in durable storage (PostgreSQL or Cassandra) so crawl can resume after restart. Scrapy's built-in JOBDIR persistence or Apache Nutch for large-scale distributed crawling.

HTTP conditional requests (If-Modified-Since, ETag/If-None-Match) reduce bandwidth and server load for incremental content monitoring. Server returns 304 Not Modified when content hasn't changed, avoiding retransmission. UNSAID: many CDNs and origin servers don't implement ETags correctly—same-content responses may generate different ETags across servers (cluster incoherence). Some servers return weak ETags (W/"abc") that indicate semantically equivalent but not byte-identical content. A practical implementation stores both ETag and Last-Modified from the previous response and sends both headers on the next request. For sites that don't support conditionals, SHA-256 hash of the response body serves as a local-side ETag. Change detection false positives from ads, timestamps, and dynamic elements require semantic-level change detection: compare extracted main content, not raw HTML bytes.

Anti-bot systems (Cloudflare, Akamai Bot Manager, DataDome) detect scraping through behavioral signals beyond simple User-Agent checking: TLS fingerprint (JA3 hash), HTTP/2 settings frames, missing browser-specific headers, mouse movement patterns, canvas fingerprint, and timing anomalies. UNSAID: changing User-Agent alone is insufficient—a Python requests library claiming to be Chrome has a different TLS fingerprint from actual Chrome. curl-cffi library impersonates Chrome's TLS fingerprint. Playwright's stealth mode (playwright-stealth) patches navigator properties but doesn't fix network-level fingerprints. The most reliable approach for difficult targets is residential proxy rotation combined with browser impersonation at the network stack level. Rate limiting your own requests to match human-plausible patterns (1-3 req/s with random jitter) is often more effective than sophisticated evasion techniques.

Named Entity Recognition (NER) and entity linking pipeline for ingested content: use spaCy's en_core_web_trf (transformer-based) for high-accuracy NER on article text, extracting PERSON, ORG, GPE, PRODUCT, EVENT entities. Link entities to knowledge bases: Wikidata Q-items via entity-fishing or BLINK (Facebook's bi-encoder model) for disambiguation (Apple the company vs apple the fruit). Store entity mentions in entity_mentions table with entity_id (Wikidata QID), mention_text, confidence, and article_id. Build entity co-occurrence graph for knowledge extraction. Batch processing optimization: process articles in batches of 32 through spaCy pipe() for 5x throughput vs item-by-item. Schedule re-linking when entity resolution models update. This pipeline enables entity-based search, topic tracking, and knowledge graph construction from unstructured news content.

Paywall detection classifies articles before attempting full extraction to avoid wasting resources on metered or hard-walled content. Signal-based classifier: check for presence of paywall CSS classes (paywall, subscription-required, premium-content), HTTP response headers (Paywall-Bypass), meta tags (og:paywall), and JSON-LD Article properties (isAccessibleForFree=false). UNSAID: many paywalls are soft (metered) and return full content in HTML but hide it with CSS—the HTML source contains the full article even if the browser shows a paywall. Content length heuristic: articles under 500 words on news sites indicate truncation. Structured data (JSON-LD) is the most reliable signal—Google's NewsArticle schema includes isAccessibleForFree field that publishers set for SEO. Archive.ph and Wayback Machine often have pre-paywall copies accessible for research purposes.

Charset detection failures corrupt multilingual content at ingestion time. HTTP Content-Type header may specify wrong charset; HTML meta charset tag overrides HTTP header per browser spec but many parsers don't implement this priority correctly; some documents have BOM (byte order mark) that indicates encoding but breaks naive string parsing. UNSAID: chardet/charset-normalizer for automatic encoding detection has 5-15% error rate on short documents—incorrect detection silently corrupts text, replacing characters with ? or mojibake (garbled multi-byte sequences). The most common failure: UTF-8 content served with Content-Type: text/html; charset=ISO-8859-1 decoded as ISO-8859-1, corrupting non-ASCII characters. The correct priority order: BOM > HTTP charset > HTML meta > autodetection > UTF-8 fallback. Always store original bytes alongside decoded text for recovery.

Topic classification pipeline for ingested articles: fine-tune a BERT-based classifier (bert-base-uncased or topic-specific checkpoint) on labeled news categories. Use hierarchical classification: coarse (politics, technology, sports) then fine-grained subcategories. Zero-shot classification (facebook/bart-large-mnli) enables new category addition without retraining—pass candidate labels as NLI hypothesis. For multilingual content: mDeBERTa-v3-base-mnli-xnli cross-lingual zero-shot. Batch classification with Hugging Face pipeline(device=0) for GPU acceleration. Cache classification results—articles don't change category. Threshold tuning: use precision-recall curve to set per-category thresholds balancing false positive and false negative rates. Store top-3 predictions with confidence for human review of borderline cases. Re-classify when model updates.

Source credibility scoring provides quality signal for downstream ranking and filtering. Signals: domain authority (Moz DA, Majestic TF), MBFC (Media Bias/Fact Check) database, NewsGuard reliability score, AllSides bias rating, publishing frequency (irregular=lower quality), edit staff size, corrections policy presence (https://example.com/corrections), HTTPS and security headers. UNSAID: credibility signals are time-stable and should be computed per-domain, not per-article, to avoid redundant computation. Automated credibility proxies (Alexa rank, backlinks count) correlate with credibility but can be gamed. Content-level signals: byline presence, quoted sources with names (not 'anonymous'), linked citations, and publication date transparency. Combine domain-level and article-level signals into composite score via logistic regression calibrated on human-labeled dataset.

robots.txt parsing errors lead to either over-crawling (ignoring restrictions) or under-crawling (incorrectly applying restrictions). Common failures: case-sensitive User-Agent matching (robots.txt is case-insensitive), misinterpreting wildcard patterns (only * is supported, not regex), ignoring Crawl-delay directive, and not refreshing robots.txt periodically (cache it for 24h per convention, not forever). UNSAID: Google's robots.txt spec differs from the original 1994 spec—Allow directive is a Google extension now widely supported but not in original spec. Sites often have multiple robots.txt at different paths; only /robots.txt at root is authoritative. Sitemap directives in robots.txt are suggestions, not permissions. Violating robots.txt has legal implications in some jurisdictions (hiQ v LinkedIn, CDK v AutoNation); always comply in commercial scraping scenarios.

Multilingual content ingestion pipeline: language detection with langdetect (based on Google's language-detection library) or fastText's lid.176.bin model (176 languages, faster). Store detected language with confidence score alongside each article. Language-specific processing pipeline: Arabic/Hebrew right-to-left text normalization, CJK (Chinese/Japanese/Korean) word segmentation (jieba for Chinese, MeCab for Japanese), Arabic diacritic normalization. Translate titles to English for cross-language deduplication using multilingual embedding models (LaBSE, paraphrase-multilingual-mpnet-base-v2). Store both original and translated versions. Language-specific stop word lists for better indexing. Elasticsearch index settings: language-specific analyzers (arabic, icu_analyzer for CJK). This enables unified search and deduplication across globally sourced content.

Publication date extraction requires multi-source reconciliation because dates appear in multiple locations with different formats and reliability levels. Extraction sources in priority order: JSON-LD datePublished/dateModified (most reliable, structured), Open Graph og:published_time og:modified_time, HTML5 <time> element with datetime attribute, meta name=pubdate/article:published_time, URL date patterns (/2024/03/15/ path segments), byline date text, HTTP Last-Modified header (least reliable—reflects server modification, not editorial publication). UNSAID: the most common failure is accepting the first found date without sanity checking—dates from future (timezone bugs), dates before the web (Unix epoch 0), and dates far in the past (content migration artifacts) all occur in practice. Valid date range check (1990 to now+24h) catches most outliers. Timezone normalization to UTC at storage time is essential for global content.

Content extraction from mobile-specific pages (/m/, AMP) duplicates articles in the corpus when both desktop and mobile versions are ingested. AMP pages have canonical link pointing to desktop version—always follow canonical to deduplicate. UNSAID: AMP pages (amp.dev) have different HTML structure and may have less content than canonical version; always prefer canonical for content extraction. Sites that redirect mobile User-Agent to m.example.com without proper canonical tags create silent duplication. Accelerated Mobile Pages inject AMP-specific attributes and components (amp-img, amp-video) that confuse non-AMP-aware extractors. URL normalization must strip AMP-specific query params (?amp=1, &amp_markup) and resolve to canonical before storage. Check both X-Frame-Options and Link rel=canonical headers.

Building a real-time news stream processing pipeline with Apache Kafka and Python: RSS/scraper workers publish raw articles to Kafka topic (raw-articles) with partition by source domain for ordering. Faust (Python stream processing framework) or Kafka Streams consumes raw-articles, applies deduplication (lookup Bloom filter in Redis), routes to per-processor topics. Processors: NLP enrichment (entities, topics, sentiment), content extraction, thumbnail download. Output to processed-articles topic consumed by storage workers writing to PostgreSQL + Elasticsearch. Dead-letter topic for failed messages with retry logic. Kafka consumer group offsets enable exactly-once processing with idempotent producers. Schema Registry (Confluent or Apicurio) enforces Avro/Protobuf schemas across producers/consumers, preventing schema drift. Monitoring: consumer lag metric is the key health indicator.

Thumbnail and image extraction from articles requires multi-fallback strategy: og:image (best—explicitly set by publisher for social sharing), twitter:card image, JSON-LD image property, article's first img src with minimum dimensions filter (exclude icons and tracking pixels). UNSAID: og:image URLs frequently expire (CDN signed URLs, temporary S3 presigned URLs), break when source site restructures, or return 403 when hotlink protection is enabled. Solution: download and re-host thumbnails at ingest time in your own CDN. Image size validation: require minimum 400x300px to exclude icons. Aspect ratio filter: exclude very narrow or very wide images (banners, ads). WebP conversion at storage time reduces bandwidth. Perceptual hash (pHash) deduplication identifies visually identical images served from different URLs.

Rate limiting coordination across distributed crawlers requires centralized state—without it, 10 concurrent workers each independently applying 1 req/s limit to a domain generates 10 req/s, likely triggering blocks. Token bucket algorithm in Redis: EVAL Lua scripts for atomic rate limit check-and-decrement. Common failure: using Redis INCR + EXPIRE for rate limiting has a race condition—INCR may succeed while EXPIRE hasn't run yet, and if the process crashes, the key persists forever. Fix: use Lua script or Redis cell module for atomic rate limiting. Per-domain adaptive rate limiting: increase delay after receiving 429, reduce after successful responses. Respect Retry-After header value from 429 responses. UNSAID: some sites serve 429 from WAF at IP level before application-level rate limits—a single slow worker generating 5 req/s can trigger IP blocks that affect all workers sharing the IP.

Elasticsearch indexing strategy for news content search: index mapping with title (text+keyword for exact and full-text), body (text with English analyzer), published_at (date), entities (nested or keyword array), source_id (keyword), language (keyword), topics (keyword array). Use index aliases for zero-downtime reindexing: write to alias, reindex to new index, atomic alias swap. Sharding: 1 shard per 20-30GB data; primary shard count fixed at creation—plan capacity ahead. Relevance tuning: boost title over body (title^3), recency boost with function_score using gauss decay on published_at. Percolation queries for real-time alerting: register saved searches as percolator documents, run incoming articles against percolator index to find matching alerts. Ingest pipeline for on-the-fly transformations (language detection, field enrichment) at index time.

Summarization as a preprocessing step for downstream tasks: extractive summarization (TextRank, LexRank) selects representative sentences without generation—100% faithfulness to source, fast (no GPU required), deterministic. Abstractive summarization (BART, T5, Pegasus) generates novel sentences—better readability but may hallucinate facts not in the source. UNSAID: for news ingestion, extractive summarization is usually preferred—it preserves factual accuracy, source attribution is clear (sentences are verbatim quotes), and it runs orders of magnitude faster than abstractive models. Hybrid approach: extractive to select top-5 sentences, then abstractive to compress those sentences reduces both hallucination risk and computational cost. Store the summary separately from full text; don't replace full text with summary for downstream search. Summary length guidance: 2-3 sentences (80-120 words) is optimal for scanning interfaces.

Concurrent article ingestion without distributed locking creates duplicate records in the database when two workers fetch the same URL simultaneously and both pass the existence check before either commits. Optimistic locking (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING) with URL unique constraint is the correct database-level solution—one insert wins, other silently discards. UNSAID: the common mistake is using SELECT then INSERT—the gap between SELECT (checking existence) and INSERT (writing record) is a TOCTOU (Time-of-Check-Time-of-Use) race condition. Application-level locking with Redis SETNX per URL prevents duplicate fetch work: workers race to acquire URL lock before fetching; loser skips the URL. Lock TTL must exceed maximum fetch+extract+store time to prevent false lock expiration. Always monitor duplicate_count metric to detect when locking mechanisms break.

Content quality scoring pipeline assigns numeric quality signal to each ingested article for downstream filtering and ranking. Signal components: article length (400-5000 words optimal range, penalize very short and very long), author byline presence (+1), publication date present (+1), image present (+0.5), linked sources within text (+1 per unique domain), code block presence for tech content (+0.5), reading level (Flesch-Kincaid 60-80 target range for general audience), HTML quality (semantic tags article/p vs div soup), duplicate title check, grammatical error density (LanguageTool API). Normalize each signal 0-1, weighted sum into composite score. Train logistic regression classifier on human-labeled quality samples using these features. Store quality score in dedicated column with component breakdown JSON for explainability and feature engineering.

Semantic deduplication using dense vector embeddings catches near-duplicate content that differ in wording—wire service articles (Reuters, AP) syndicated to hundreds of outlets with minor edits. Encode article titles + first paragraph with sentence-transformers (all-MiniLM-L6-v2 for speed, all-mpnet-base-v2 for accuracy), compute cosine similarity. Threshold 0.92+ indicates near-duplicate. ANN (Approximate Nearest Neighbor) search via FAISS or Annoy avoids quadratic pairwise comparison: add new article embedding to index, query k=20 nearest neighbors, flag pairs above threshold. UNSAID: semantic deduplication must run within temporal windows (articles within 24 hours of each other) to avoid false positives from recurring topics—two articles about 'Apple quarterly earnings' from different quarters are NOT duplicates despite high semantic similarity. Cluster duplicates and select canonical (highest quality score or earliest publication).

HTTP redirect chains during crawling cause subtle content misattribution and URL tracking issues. A URL that 301-redirects through a tracking redirect (outbrain.com/redirect?to=target.com) before reaching the canonical URL stores the redirect chain URL, not the canonical. UNSAID: always follow redirects to final destination and store that as the canonical URL, but limit redirect depth to prevent infinite loops (max 5-10 redirects). Redirects that resolve to login pages (302 to /login) indicate paywalled content but aren't always distinguishable from legitimate redirects programmatically—check final URL path for /login, /signin, /subscribe patterns. Meta refresh redirects (<meta http-equiv=refresh>) require HTML parsing to follow and are invisible to HTTP-level redirect following. httpx/aiohttp follows redirects by default; track original URL and final URL separately in storage.

Building a news alerting system on ingested content: users define alert queries (keywords, entity names, topics) stored in alerts table. Incoming articles matched against active alerts using Elasticsearch percolation or PostgreSQL full-text search: SELECT alert_id FROM alerts WHERE to_tsvector('english', query) @@ to_tsquery($article_entities). Alert delivery pipeline: triggered alerts queued for batching (immediate, hourly digest, daily digest based on user preference). Deduplication: don't alert on same story from multiple sources—cluster related articles within 2-hour window, send single alert with top article. Alert fatigue prevention: rate limit alert frequency per user (max 10/day), track dismissal patterns to auto-adjust threshold. Store alert_trigger_log for debugging and user engagement analytics. Email templates with entity highlighting and read-more links drive re-engagement.

Structured data extraction from HTML using JSON-LD, Microdata, and RDFa provides machine-readable metadata that's more reliable than heuristic extraction. json-ld.org extractor libraries parse schema.org annotations: Article, NewsArticle, Event, Product, Person types contain structured author, date, description fields. UNSAID: JSON-LD is the most reliable—it's separate from visual layout, parseable with standard JSON parser after extraction from <script type=application/ld+json>. Microdata is interleaved with HTML (itemprop, itemscope attributes)—parseable but fragile if HTML structure changes. Many sites have invalid or incomplete JSON-LD that requires tolerant parsing (try/except around JSON.loads). Google's Rich Results Test reveals what structured data a page actually exposes. Combining JSON-LD with OpenGraph creates robust metadata extraction with automatic fallback.

Infinite scroll and pagination require different crawl strategies, and misidentifying the pattern causes incomplete content collection. Traditional pagination: follow next-page links in HTML. AJAX pagination: network tab reveals XHR API calls with page/cursor parameters that can be called directly. Infinite scroll with cursor: requires tracking cursor from last response and constructing next request. UNSAID: attempting to crawl infinite scroll by scrolling the page in headless browser is unreliable—page.evaluate() to scroll then waiting for network idle works but is 100x slower than direct API calls. Termination detection is difficult—API endpoints may return empty data, reduced data, or HTTP 204 to signal end. Some APIs have maximum offset limits (Elasticsearch default 10,000 records)—cursor-based APIs are better for deep pagination. Always verify pagination completeness by comparing scraped count against article counter displayed on page.

Citation and reference extraction from articles enables knowledge graph construction and source reliability assessment. Hyperlink extraction: collect all <a href> links from article body, filter external links (different domain), classify link type (source citation, related article, advertisement). For academic-style footnotes: match [1], [2] pattern text to reference list at page bottom. DOI resolver: detect DOIs in text (10.xxxx/xxxx pattern), resolve via doi.org API to structured bibliographic data. UNSAID: Wikipedia-style citations use <ref> tags; news articles use inline hyperlinks instead. Link text quality signals: anchor text 'source', 'according to', 'study' indicates citation; 'click here', 'read more' indicates navigational link. Store citation graph (article→cited_article) for PageRank-style authority scoring. Cross-source citation analysis identifies which outlets are primary sources vs secondary republishers.

Content freshness management balances re-crawl frequency against crawl budget and server politeness. Optimal re-crawl interval varies by content type: breaking news sites (every 5-15 minutes), daily newspapers (every 2-4 hours), weekly magazines (daily), static reference content (weekly or change-triggered). UNSAID: re-crawling unchanged content wastes bandwidth—HTTP 304 Not Modified responses from conditional GET requests signal 'no change' efficiently but require server support. Content change frequency estimation using historical change data (how often has this URL changed before?) creates an adaptive scheduler. Sitemaps with <lastmod> and <changefreq> elements are publisher-provided hints for crawl frequency—follow them when available. Priority queue based on source domain's news velocity (articles-per-day metric) ensures high-activity sources are checked more frequently than low-activity ones.

Sentiment analysis on news content produces misleading results when applied at article level rather than sentence/entity level. An article with neutral headline but negative body sentiment scores neutrally when title is overweighted—but topic models trained on article-level sentiment inherit this noise. UNSAID: entity-level sentiment (how does this article portray Elon Musk?) requires aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA), not document-level sentiment. Generic sentiment models (VADER, TextBlob) were trained on social media/reviews and perform poorly on formal news writing—specialized models like FinBERT (financial news) outperform general models by 15-30%. Sentiment in direct quotes reflects the quoted speaker's sentiment, not the article's perspective—separate direct quote extraction from sentiment analysis. Negation handling is a common failure: 'not surprising' is NOT positive even though 'surprising' by itself might be.

Social media signal enrichment for ingested articles: fetch share counts and engagement metrics from Twitter/X API v2 (public metrics endpoint), Facebook Graph API (og_object shares), Reddit search API (find posts linking to URL). Normalize engagement scores across platforms (Reddit upvotes != Facebook shares in magnitude). Store metrics in time-series table for trend analysis—viral articles show exponential growth in first 2 hours. Cache social metrics with TTL of 15 minutes for recently published articles, 1 hour for day-old articles. UNSAID: Facebook deprecated the public share count API in 2018; unofficial scraping methods are unreliable and ToS-violating. Sharing Intent API (now removed) alternatives include Crowdtangle (media companies only) and News API partners. Focus on available signals: Reddit discussion count, Twitter public search (rate limited at 500k tweets/month on Basic tier), and Hacker News API (fully open).

Content extraction accuracy measurement requires ground truth dataset and reproducible evaluation. Build evaluation corpus: 500+ manually annotated articles from diverse domains with correct title, author, date, body, and thumbnail. Evaluate extraction pipeline on held-out test set with F1 score per field. ROUGE-L score for body extraction measures overlap with reference text. Common quality dimensions: precision (no boilerplate in extracted body), recall (no missing paragraphs), boundary accuracy (starts and ends at correct position). UNSAID: evaluation must include adversarial examples—articles with unusual layouts, minimal boilerplate ratio, navigation-heavy pages, and content-type edge cases. Regression test: any extraction change must not decrease F1 on existing test set. Track extraction accuracy by domain to identify sites with poor extraction quality for targeted rule creation.

PDF content extraction loses critical formatting information—tables become streams of numbers, footnotes become inline text, multi-column layouts read left-to-right across columns (mixing column 1 and column 2 text). pdfminer.six, PyMuPDF, and Camelot handle different PDF structures with different success rates—no single tool extracts all PDF types correctly. UNSAID: PDFs are fundamentally a page description language, not a document structure format—extractors infer structure from visual position, which breaks for scanned PDFs (requires OCR, Tesseract), PDFs with complex layouts, and digitally-signed PDFs with restricted extraction permissions. For research papers, better to use paper API (Semantic Scholar, arXiv) to get structured text than extracting from PDF. Store original PDF alongside extracted text for re-extraction with improved tools. PDFs with embedded fonts that aren't standard may render incorrectly or extract garbled text.

Building a knowledge graph from ingested news articles: entities (people, organizations, locations, events) as nodes, relationships (CEO_OF, LOCATED_IN, PARTICIPATED_IN, MENTIONED_WITH) as edges. Extract relationships using Stanford OpenIE or ReVerb for open information extraction, or fine-tuned REBEL model for structured relation extraction. Store in graph database (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune) for relationship traversal queries. Co-occurrence-based relationships: entities mentioned in same sentence have implicit relationship. Event extraction: ACE-style event detection identifies event triggers and participant roles (Agent, Patient, Location, Time). UNSAID: knowledge graphs from news require continuous maintenance—entities merge, split, get renamed (Twitter→X), and relationships become historical (was-CEO-of). Temporal knowledge graphs annotate edges with valid_from and valid_to timestamps for accurate historical queries.

Incremental indexing to Elasticsearch vs full reindex: incremental indexing (update/upsert individual documents) is faster but allows index drift over time if updates fail silently. Full reindex periodically corrects drift but is resource-intensive. The hybrid pattern: incremental for real-time freshness, weekly full reindex for consistency. Elasticsearch bulk API batches 100-1000 documents per request, reducing overhead. UNSAID: Elasticsearch's _update_by_query and delete_by_query run in background and return immediately—queries continue running after HTTP response; don't assume completion. Version-based optimistic concurrency: include if_seq_no and if_primary_term in index requests to prevent lost updates from concurrent writes. Monitor index health: yellow (replica not assigned) is expected in single-node development but problematic in production.

API-based news ingestion (NewsAPI, GDELT, New York Times API, Guardian API) has undocumented rate limits, pagination limits, and content restrictions that differ from documented limits. NewsAPI free tier limits content to 24-hour window and 100 results per search—older content requires paid plans. GDELT 2.0 allows 250 queries/24 hours and returns only 75 most recent articles per query. UNSAID: API responses often exclude full article body—only headlines, descriptions, and URLs are returned; full content requires separate fetch to source URL (subject to paywalls and robots.txt). API coverage is uneven—major English-language outlets are overrepresented, local and non-English sources underrepresented. Always combine API sources with direct crawling for comprehensive coverage. Store API source and API response timestamp separately from content publication timestamp.

Monitoring and observability for content ingestion pipelines: instrument with Prometheus metrics—articles_ingested_total (counter by source), extraction_duration_seconds (histogram), duplicate_ratio (gauge), failed_fetches_total (counter by error_type). Grafana dashboards showing ingestion rate over time, failure rate by domain, queue depth, and processing latency. Alert rules: ingestion_rate < threshold triggers (dead pipeline detection), failure_rate > 20% for a domain triggers (domain-specific investigation). Structured logging with correlation_id linking all log lines for a single article through the pipeline. Distributed tracing (Jaeger/Zipkin) for latency analysis across microservices. Dead-letter queue monitoring: articles stuck in retry queue beyond 24 hours signal systemic issues. Business KPI dashboards: unique sources active today, articles per topic per day for editorial visibility.

HTML main content detection algorithms model the problem differently: DOM-tree-based (Justext, Boilerplate Remover) classify blocks by text density and link density; readability-based (Mozilla Readability, newspaper3k) score elements by article likelihood using heuristics; ML-based (trafilatura's LXML + XGBoost, DragNet) learn from labeled corpora. UNSAID: no single algorithm handles all site layouts—combination approaches (consensus voting across extractors, fallback hierarchy) outperform any single extractor on diverse corpora. Maintenance burden differs: rule-based extractors break when sites redesign; ML extractors degrade gradually as distribution shifts and require periodic retraining. trafilatura outperforms newspaper3k and Readability on news sites in benchmarks while being actively maintained. Site-specific CSS selectors still outperform all generic extractors for high-value sources where precision matters.

IP-based blocking occurs when a single IP sends excessive requests to a target site—typical block threshold is 100-500 req/hour for residential IPs, 10-50 req/hour for datacenter IPs. UNSAID: datacenter IPs (AWS, GCP, Azure, DigitalOcean) are pre-blacklisted by major anti-bot services—requests from these IPs trigger aggressive bot scoring regardless of request rate. Residential proxy rotation (Bright Data, Oxylabs, SOAX) uses IPs assigned to home users, which receive lower bot scores. Rotating User-Agent without rotating IP is ineffective—consistent IP with changing User-Agent is a strong bot signal. IP reputation services (IPQualityScore, MaxMind) rate IPs for bot probability; sites query these at request time. Monitor for soft blocks: requests that succeed with HTTP 200 but return empty content, CAPTCHAs, or login walls—not all blocks return 403 or 429.

Byline and author extraction pipeline: primary source JSON-LD author property (most reliable), OpenGraph article:author, schema.org Person linked from rel=author, <meta name=author>, byline heuristics (text starting with 'By ', 'Written by', 'Reported by', preceded by author section class). Clean extracted name: remove title prefixes ('Staff Writer John Smith'→'John Smith'), affiliation suffixes ('John Smith, Reuters'→'John Smith', store 'Reuters' as affiliation). Author disambiguation: same name across different publications may be different people—don't auto-merge author identities. Build author profile: aggregate articles by author, compute topics, publication frequency, publication outlets. Store as Person entity linked from articles. UNSAID: many aggregators and wire service articles have no individual author byline—store 'Editorial' or source name as author rather than null.

Cold storage and retrieval optimization for archived news content: use Apache Parquet with Snappy compression to store articles in columnar format achieving 5-10x compression vs JSON. Partition by publication_date (year/month) and language for efficient time-range and language queries. Store in S3-compatible object storage with DuckDB for SQL queries directly against Parquet files without loading into database: SELECT * FROM read_parquet('s3://bucket/articles/2024/**/*.parquet') WHERE source_domain='nytimes.com'. UNSAID: Parquet's columnar format excels at analytical queries (select few columns from many rows) but is inefficient for random row access by ID—maintain PostgreSQL index for ID-based lookups while storing bulk content in Parquet. Arrow format enables zero-copy reads between Python processes and DuckDB, critical for ML feature extraction pipelines that process billions of tokens.

Async HTTP request batching with aiohttp/httpx can overwhelm targets when concurrency is set too high—100 concurrent connections to a single news site triggers aggressive rate limiting or IP bans within minutes. UNSAID: asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency_limit) controls maximum concurrent requests, but this is a global limit; per-domain concurrency limits are more precise. Using asyncio.gather() without a semaphore creates as many concurrent connections as there are URLs in the batch—unbounded parallelism. Connection pooling (aiohttp.TCPConnector(limit_per_host=5)) controls concurrent connections per hostname at the HTTP level. DNS resolution caching: aiohttp resolves DNS per-connection by default—use TTLCache for DNS results to avoid DNS lookup overhead at scale. For 10K+ URLs/hour, batch into domain-partitioned queues where each domain queue respects its crawl delay independently.

Event-driven content processing with AWS Lambda and SQS: S3 event triggers Lambda on new raw HTML upload, Lambda extracts content and writes to processed/ prefix, S3 event triggers NLP Lambda for entity extraction. SQS FIFO queue ensures per-source ordering for deduplication. Lambda cold starts affect latency for infrequent sources—use Provisioned Concurrency for latency-sensitive paths. SQS visibility timeout must exceed Lambda function max duration; Lambda DLQ (dead-letter queue) captures failed invocations. Batch window configuration aggregates multiple SQS messages per Lambda invocation (up to 300 seconds or 10 messages), improving throughput. UNSAID: Lambda's 15-minute timeout is insufficient for large page JavaScript rendering with Playwright—containerize long-running extraction in ECS Fargate or EC2 spot instances for Playwright-dependent workloads.

Content licensing and copyright implications govern what can be stored and how it can be used for AI training. UNSAID: scraping publicly accessible content is generally permitted for non-commercial research under fair use (US) and similar exceptions (EU Article 4 TDM exception). However, training commercial AI models on scraped content is contested—NYT v. OpenAI, Authors Guild v. OpenAI establish ongoing legal uncertainty. robots.txt restrictions for AI training (User-agent: GPTBot, CCBot) are increasingly common but not legally binding in all jurisdictions. Attribution preservation (storing source URL, author, publication) is both legally prudent and technically necessary for RAG systems to cite sources. Creative Commons licensed content (CC-BY, CC-SA) allows reuse with attribution—programmatically filter by CC license from Creative Commons API or license schema.org property.

Cookie consent banners block content extraction in EU-targeted sites—clicking 'Accept All' before scraping is required, but consent state persists via cookies that must be stored and replayed in subsequent requests. UNSAID: Playwright page.click() on GDPR consent banners is fragile—banners use different button text, IDs, and structure across CMPs (OneTrust, Quantcast Choice, Didomi). playwright-stealth doesn't bypass consent walls. A practical approach: use playwright-bypass-csp or inject consent cookies directly for known CMP patterns (OneTrust: Set-Cookie with OptanonConsent=; Quantcast: Set-Cookie with eupubconsent-v2=). Some sites block all traffic from EU IP ranges to non-consenting users—using non-EU proxy IPs bypasses consent requirement entirely. Always document which sites require consent cookie injection and maintain the cookie values updated as CMPs version their formats.

Scrapy spider with middleware stack for production news crawling: RotatingProxyMiddleware handles proxy rotation with per-domain ban detection, RetryMiddleware with exponential backoff for transient failures, UserAgentMiddleware rotates UA strings from browser-realistic list, RedirectMiddleware follows canonical redirects, CacheMiddleware stores responses for replay during development. Pipeline: ContentExtractionPipeline applies trafilatura+site-specific rules, QualityFilterPipeline drops low-quality articles (< 200 words, no date), DuplicatesPipeline checks Bloom filter, PostgreSQLPipeline stores to database. ITEM_PIPELINES order matters—duplicate check before storage is critical. Settings: CONCURRENT_REQUESTS_PER_DOMAIN=2, DOWNLOAD_DELAY=1.5, RANDOMIZE_DOWNLOAD_DELAY=True. Scrapy-Playwright integration: DOWNLOAD_HANDLERS={'https': ScrapyPlaywrightDownloadHandler} routes JS-requiring URLs to Playwright.

Data provenance tracking enables debugging, auditing, and reprocessing. Each stored article should record: source_url, canonical_url, fetch_timestamp, raw_html_s3_key, extraction_version, processor_versions (NLP model versions), ingestion_job_id. UNSAID: when extraction bugs are found, raw HTML stored in S3 allows reprocessing without re-crawling—critical for fixing bugs that affected millions of articles. Extraction version column enables SQL queries like 'articles extracted with old algorithm that need reprocessing.' Immutable event log pattern: append-only ingestion_events table records all state transitions (fetched, extracted, enriched, indexed, failed) with timestamp for each article. This log is the source of truth for debugging, auditing, and metrics. When production incidents occur, event log enables reconstruction of exactly what happened to a specific article at each step.

JavaScript Single Page Applications (SPAs) that use hash-based routing (#/article/123) are not crawlable by standard HTTP clients because the fragment identifier (#) is never sent to the server—each URL resolves to the same index.html and client-side routing renders content. UNSAID: sites that implement hashbang URLs (#!/article/123) historically expected crawlers to convert to ?_escaped_fragment_= query parameter, but Google deprecated this in 2015. Modern SPAs should use History API (pushState) URLs for crawlability, but many don't. Detection: if all article URLs in sitemap.xml contain # fragments, the site uses hash routing. Solution: headless browser rendering to capture JavaScript-rendered content. Some SPAs serve different content to crawlers vs browsers via User-Agent detection—test with Googlebot UA to get the server-rendered version if available.

Hybrid push/pull ingestion architecture: pull tier polls RSS feeds and sitemaps on schedule; push tier receives webhooks from publishers (PubSubHubbub/WebSub protocol, RSS feeds with pubsubhubbub hub link). WebSub: subscribe to hub with callback URL, hub sends POST to callback URL when feed updates. This reduces latency from 15-minute poll interval to sub-second for WebSub-enabled feeds. NewsAPI webhooks and similar paid news APIs support push delivery. IFTTT webhooks and Make.com scenarios can relay social media posts to ingestion endpoint. UNSAID: WebSub subscription must be renewed (re-subscribe before lease expiry, typically 10-30 days). Fallback poll if no WebSub notification received within 2x expected publication interval. Most major news sites don't support WebSub—pull remains the primary mechanism with push providing supplementary real-time layer.

Toxic content detection and content moderation at ingestion is required for platforms that surface content to users. Multi-layer approach: keyword blocklist (fast, low cost, high false positive rate), ML classifier (Perspective API, Detoxify, OpenAI Moderation API) for nuanced detection, human review queue for borderline cases. Category detection: hate speech, violence, adult content, spam, misinformation. UNSAID: news articles legitimately discuss toxic content (reporting on hate crimes, extremist groups) without being toxic themselves—context matters. Simple keyword matching flags journalism about sensitive topics. Perspective API's TOXICITY score is a probability across categories; threshold tuning is domain-dependent (news platform tolerates higher discussion of violence than children's platform). Store classification results with model version for re-evaluation when models improve. Never permanently delete flagged content without human review for legal/archive compliance.

XML sitemap processing failures are common due to sitemap format variations: sitemap index files reference child sitemaps (recursive download required), compressed sitemaps (.xml.gz) need decompression before parsing, news sitemaps (Google News Sitemap extension) have different schema from standard sitemaps. UNSAID: sitemaps can reference millions of URLs—naive in-memory parsing of a 50MB sitemap XML exhausts Python memory. Use iterative XML parsing (lxml iterparse, xml.etree.ElementTree iterparse) for memory-efficient streaming. Sitemap <lastmod> dates are often inaccurate (CMS bugs, bulk updates) and shouldn't be trusted for freshness detection. Sitemaps may include URLs for pagination, tag pages, and non-article content—URL path pattern filtering (/article/, /story/, /news/, /post/) extracts article-specific URLs. Some sites require authentication to access sitemaps even if sitemap is linked from robots.txt.

Building a media monitoring competitive intelligence system: define tracked brands/entities as watch terms, match against ingested article titles and first paragraphs using Elasticsearch percolation. Deduplicate mentions of same story (cluster by semantic similarity + 24h window). Score mention sentiment per entity using ABSA (aspect-based sentiment analysis). Track mention frequency over time: daily/weekly brand mention volume. Share of Voice: brand_mentions / total_mentions_in_category. Competitive comparison dashboard: side-by-side entity mention trends. Alert on mention spike (>2 standard deviations above 30-day baseline). Source breakdown: which publications mention brand most (positive/negative ratio per source). UNSAID: mention volume alone is misleading—distinguish PR-driven coverage (press releases) from organic journalism and editorial coverage for accurate competitive intelligence.

Proxy pool management requires active health monitoring and rotation strategy. Proxy health check: periodic GET request to a known-clean URL, measure success rate and latency, flag proxies with >5% failure rate or >3s latency as degraded. Per-domain proxy assignment: assign specific proxy pool segments to specific target domains to avoid cross-contamination when one domain bans a proxy. UNSAID: proxy providers oversell their pools—advertised pool size doesn't equal usable pool size because many IPs are already banned by major targets. Sticky session proxies (same IP for sequence of requests from same session) are required for sites with session-based bot detection that track IP consistency. Rotating proxies for every request works for stateless content but fails for multi-step authenticated scraping. Track ban rate per proxy and per domain pair to optimize routing and identify high-value IPs worth preserving.

Author extraction from multi-author articles and collaborative content pieces is commonly flawed—extractors that find the first author name stop and miss co-authors. Scientific articles and investigative journalism regularly have 2-10 authors. UNSAID: author lists in JSON-LD may be an array of Person objects or a single string with comma-separated names—handle both formats. Wire service articles (Reuters, AP) list agency name as author without individual bylines; don't discard these as invalid. 'Staff' and 'Editorial Board' are valid author values for institutional authorship. Cross-publication author disambiguation is hard—'John Smith' at NYT is different from 'John Smith' at Guardian—avoid auto-merging without email or social profile linking. Author affiliation changes over time (journalist switches outlet)—byline extraction must store publication date context to link author-affiliation relationships temporally.

Geolocation enrichment for ingested articles: extract location entities from NER, geocode to coordinates using OpenStreetMap Nominatim (free, rate-limited) or Google Places API (paid, more accurate). Cache geocoding results by place name to avoid redundant API calls. Reverse geocode article origin IP (for web sources) to get server location—proxy for editorial geography. Store bounding box for regions and polygons for countries/states for spatial queries. PostGIS enabled PostgreSQL: query articles mentioning locations within 100km radius: WHERE ST_DWithin(geocoded_location::geography, ST_MakePoint(lng,lat)::geography, 100000). UNSAID: geocoding ambiguity (Paris, Texas vs Paris, France) requires context disambiguation—entity linker that resolves to Wikidata items disambiguates by population and article topic context. Store Wikidata QID alongside coordinates for interoperability with knowledge graph.

Temporal trend analysis on ingested content requires time-series data modeling decisions at schema design time. Storing a single published_at timestamp enables basic time filtering but not event clustering. UNSAID: 'news storm' detection (rapid increase in articles about a topic in a short window) requires aggregation at 1-hour or 15-minute granularity—precompute these time-series aggregates as materialized views or time-series database entries. TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL extension) adds time-series hypertables with automatic partitioning and time-bucket() aggregation function. Trend analysis: Z-score of topic frequency vs rolling 30-day baseline identifies statistically significant spikes. Momentum metric: articles-per-hour rate of change. 'Topic decay curve' models how long topics stay in news—most topics decay to baseline within 72 hours, useful for article expiration policies.

Named entity recognition models trained on newswire text (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes) underperform on informal web content, social media quotes within articles, and non-English text. UNSAID: NER models don't know about entities created after their training cutoff—a company that IPO'd last month, a person who became famous recently, a new geopolitical entity (South Sudan in 2011) are all 'unknown' to older models. Entity linking to Wikidata handles this better if the entity has been added to Wikidata. Zero-shot NER with Generative Models (asking LLM to extract entities) handles novel entities but is 100x more expensive and slower than fine-tuned models. For production, fine-tune NER models on domain-specific data quarterly to capture entity drift. Track precision/recall on held-out test set after each fine-tune to detect regression.

Building a fact-check and misinformation detection system on ingested content: integrate with fact-checking databases (Google Fact Check Tools API, ClaimBuster API, Snopes/PolitiFact search). ClaimBuster scores claim check-worthiness (0-1 scale). For automated fact-checking: extract claims from article text, query evidence databases, compare with known fact-check verdicts. Cross-reference article claims against Wikipedia for named fact verification. UNSAID: automated fact-checking is an unsolved research problem—current systems have 60-70% accuracy at best and are unreliable for nuanced claims. Use these signals as flags for human review, not automated verdicts. Misinformation network detection: identify articles sharing the same debunked narratives across different sites using semantic similarity clustering. Flag for human review rather than automated suppression to avoid censorship concerns.

Incremental deduplication strategy for a growing corpus: new articles should be checked against recent content only (last 14-30 days for news deduplication) using time-windowed similarity search, not against the entire historical corpus. UNSAID: checking every new article against millions of historical articles is computationally prohibitive—index time-windowed embeddings in FAISS with periodic eviction of old vectors. Exact duplicate detection (same URL or same fingerprint) should use hash lookup (O(1)), not vector search. Near-duplicate detection (semantic similarity) needs approximate nearest neighbor only within the temporal window. Wire service articles get recycled months later with same content and new headline—similarity threshold should be high (0.95+) or use content-hash comparison rather than semantic similarity for exact duplicate variants. Deduplication serves different downstream purposes: news aggregator (hide duplicates), ML training (prevent data leakage), analytics (accurate trend metrics).

Scrapy spiders that raise exceptions in parse() callbacks silently drop items without any indication in logs unless SPIDER_MIDDLEWARES includes a proper error-logging middleware. The default behavior swallows AttributeError on missing HTML elements—if the site HTML changes and a CSS selector returns None, .text raises AttributeError which Scrapy catches and silently discards the item without logging. UNSAID: always check selector results for None before calling .text or .get() methods; use .get(default='') as safe fallback. Monitor ITEM_DROPPED signals and item_error signals in Scrapy's stats collector. Log total items scraped vs total items found in source—a discrepancy reveals silent drops. Spider contract tests (@scrapes, @returns) validate spider output structure during development. Production spider monitoring: compare today's item count to moving average; alert if drops >30%.

Building personalized content recommendation on ingested articles: collaborative filtering (user-article interaction matrix, SVD or ALS factorization) for users with history; content-based filtering (article embedding similarity to previously read articles) for new users. Hybrid approach: blend collaborative and content scores with learnable weight. Feature engineering for content-based: topic vector, entity vector, publication_date recency score, source diversity score (avoid same source saturation). Online learning: update user embeddings incrementally with each interaction (read, click, share, skip). UNSAID: recency bias is critical for news—content-based models trained only on interaction history will recommend stale articles that match old reading patterns. Inject freshness score as multiplicative factor. A/B testing recommendation algorithms requires careful instrumentation: measure engagement, click-through, read-completion, and return visit rate as composite business metric.

Database schema design for a news content store must balance write performance, query flexibility, and storage efficiency. Core table: articles (id, url, canonical_url, title, body_text, body_html, author_ids, source_id, published_at, fetched_at, language, quality_score, is_duplicate, cluster_id). Separate tables: article_entities (many-to-many), article_topics, article_images. UNSAID: storing body_html alongside body_text enables re-extraction if extraction logic improves without re-crawling. UUID vs sequential ID: UUID avoids ID enumeration attacks but is 4x slower for index range scans—use ULID (sortable UUID) for combined benefits. Partial indexes save storage: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY ON articles(published_at) WHERE quality_score > 0.7 indexes only high-quality articles. JSONB for metadata allows schema evolution without migrations—store variable metadata (social counts, API-specific fields) in metadata JSONB column.

Headless browser memory leaks in long-running Playwright/Puppeteer processes cause worker crashes after hours or days. Each unclosed browser context leaks memory; pages not explicitly closed accumulate. UNSAID: always use context manager pattern: async with playwright.chromium.launch() as browser: async with browser.new_context() as context: for clean resource lifecycle. Playwright process pool with supervisor restart on memory threshold is safer than single long-running instance. Chrome renderer processes for complex pages (lots of JavaScript) consume 200-500MB per tab—limiting concurrent pages to 3-5 per Chrome instance prevents OOM. Browser.close() must be called even on exception—use try/finally or context managers. Worker health check: monitor process memory usage in Prometheus, restart workers that exceed 2GB RSS. Docker memory limits (--memory=3g) provide hard ceiling preventing host OOM killer from killing other services.

Implementing content expiration and freshness management: articles decay in relevance over time; implement a freshness score function: score = base_quality * exp(-decay_rate * hours_since_published). Half-life varies by content type: breaking news (4-8 hours), analysis (48 hours), evergreen content (never). Store and update freshness_score column with periodic batch recalculation or TTL-based expiry in Redis for hot cache. Archive expired articles to cold storage (S3 Parquet) but retain in database with is_archived flag for historical queries. Index pruning: remove expired articles from Elasticsearch index to keep search results fresh—use Elasticsearch ILM (Index Lifecycle Management) to automatically move old indices to frozen tier. Differentiate 'expired for feed' from 'expired for search'—older articles still relevant for historical search.

Text normalization preprocessing for NLP tasks must be carefully scoped—aggressive normalization that's appropriate for sentiment analysis destroys information needed for named entity recognition. NLP pipeline preprocessing decisions: lowercase (generally yes, but preserves CAPS emphasis for sentiment), remove punctuation (yes for topic modeling, no for NER), stop word removal (yes for TF-IDF, no for language modeling), stemming vs lemmatization (stemming is faster, lemmatization more accurate), unicode normalization (NFC form recommended for consistency). UNSAID: HTML entity decoding (&→&, "→", <→<) must happen before NLP processing—raw HTML entities in text cause tokenization errors. Normalize quotation marks (“” to ASCII quotes), en/em dashes to hyphens for consistent tokenization. Store both raw and normalized text—normalization is lossy and assumptions change with new models.

Connection pooling misconfiguration in high-concurrency ingestion causes 'too many connections' errors at the PostgreSQL server. Each async worker maintains a connection pool—10 workers × 20 connections = 200 connections, potentially exceeding PostgreSQL's default max_connections=100. UNSAID: use PgBouncer as connection pooler between application and PostgreSQL, allowing hundreds of application connections to multiplex over a smaller number of actual PostgreSQL server connections. PgBouncer transaction mode releases server connection back to pool after each transaction—incompatible with LISTEN/NOTIFY and prepared statements that require session-scoped connections. Pool sizing formula: connections_per_worker = total_db_connections / num_workers, where total_db_connections = PostgreSQL max_connections × 0.8. Monitor pool saturation with asyncpg pool.get_size() and pool.get_idle_size() metrics.

Building a self-healing crawl system: implement health checks that run every 5 minutes verifying ingestion throughput. If ingestion rate drops below threshold: (1) check source accessibility with probe requests, (2) verify queue workers are running, (3) test database write performance, (4) check proxy pool health. Automated recovery actions: restart failed workers (supervisord restart), rotate to backup proxy pool, fall back to cached content for temporarily unreachable sources. Circuit breaker per source domain: after 5 consecutive failures, mark source as CIRCUIT_OPEN and skip for 30 minutes before retry. Source health dashboard: availability percentage per source over 30-day rolling window. UNSAID: distinguish between source-side failures (site down, blocking) and infrastructure failures (DNS, network, database)—automated recovery only for infrastructure failures; source-side requires different remediation strategy.

Embedding generation for content retrieval at scale: batch encode articles at ingestion time using sentence-transformers running on GPU for throughput (1000 articles/sec on A10G vs 50 articles/sec on CPU). Store embeddings as FLOAT4 arrays in PostgreSQL pgvector extension or Pinecone/Weaviate/Qdrant vector databases. pgvector index types: IVFFlat (faster build, lower recall) vs HNSW (slower build, higher recall, no reindexing needed). UNSAID: embedding models have token limits (typically 512 tokens)—long articles must be chunked and aggregated (mean pooling of chunk embeddings) for full-article representation. Embedding model choice affects downstream task performance—evaluate on your domain before deploying: multilingual models (LaBSE) for international content, domain-specific models for technical niches. Update embeddings when model is updated—version embeddings in the vector store.

gzip/deflate/brotli content encoding handling in custom HTTP clients causes garbled response text when decompression is missing or wrong. HTTPX and aiohttp auto-decompress by default, but requests library requires decode_content=True in response.iter_content(). UNSAID: some servers send incorrect Content-Encoding header (claiming gzip when content is not gzipped) causing decompression failures. Fallback strategy: try decompress, if DecompressionError then use raw bytes. Chunked transfer encoding (Transfer-Encoding: chunked) must be reassembled before content-type decoding—standard HTTP clients handle this automatically but custom parsers often don't. Brotli (br) encoding requires brotli Python package—missing package causes requests to reject br-encoded responses with 406 Not Acceptable or silently return encoded bytes. Always add Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate header to requests to hint preferred encoding.

Multi-source entity resolution system for ingested news: creates unified entity registry from mentions across all ingested articles. Blocking strategies reduce comparison space: compare entities only within same type (PERSON, ORG) and first-letter buckets. Pairwise similarity: name similarity (Jaro-Winkler + alias matching), co-mention similarity (entities that appear together frequently are likely different people). Clustering: connected components where similarity > threshold form entity clusters. Human review queue for borderline clusters. Entity canonicalization: choose most common name form as canonical, store all aliases. Entity profile: aggregate all articles mentioning entity with sentiment per article, topic associations, temporal activity. UNSAID: entity resolution must handle name changes (company rebranding: Facebook→Meta, Twitter→X) with temporal validity tracking for historical queries.

LLM-based content enrichment at scale requires careful cost optimization. Per-article LLM processing at GPT-4 prices (~$0.03/article at 1000 tokens) costs $30K for 1M articles—prohibitive for large corpora. Hybrid approach: use small fine-tuned models for classification and extraction (topic, entities, sentiment) at 1% of the cost; reserve LLM for high-value enrichment (summarization, relationship extraction, claim detection) on filtered subset (high-quality, high-engagement articles). Batch API endpoints (OpenAI Batch API, Anthropic) offer 50% cost reduction for asynchronous workloads that tolerate 24-hour latency. UNSAID: few-shot prompting with 3-5 examples outperforms zero-shot for structured extraction tasks while keeping token count manageable. Prompt caching (Anthropic's cache_control) for shared system prompt across millions of articles saves 90% of system prompt tokens after first request.

TLS certificate verification must never be disabled in production HTTP clients—urllib3's verify=False or aiohttp's ssl=False exposes all HTTPS requests to man-in-the-middle attacks. UNSAID: the common reason for disabling verification is encountering self-signed certificates on internal services or expired certificates on poorly maintained sites—fix the certificate, don't disable verification. For internal services with custom CA: add custom CA bundle using cafile parameter or REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE environment variable.
